text: Photon Bridge BREAKTHROUGH TOUR 2026 : We visited Photon Bridge in  hashtag #Eindhoven in early July, ten months after our first chat at the ECOC Exhibition in Copenhagen. What we saw at their business office on the campus of Eindhoven University of Technology was proof of something the industry doubted was possible: passive self-alignment of indium phosphide lasers onto silicon photonic chips, at insertion losses below 0.6 dB.  Here is why that matters. Combining III-V material, which generates light, with silicon photonics, which routes it cheaply at scale, has always meant trading performance for manufacturability. PhotonBridge's fix: a MEMS structure of flexible cantilevers that lets the III-V die find its own position on the silicon wafer. No active alignment, no per-chip tuning.  CTO Rui Santos showed us wafer-level testing where every chip, InP and silicon, is measured before assembly. That known-good-die approach is the real yield edge over monolithic integration or transfer-printing rivals: separate the problem, test each piece, combine only what works.  Product one: an ITLA for coherent data center links, already beating the 1.6 terabit spec at under 2 watts.  Product two: an external laser source for AI interconnects, targeting 38-40 milliwatts per colour with OSFP MSA compatibility across several generations. CEO Paul Marchal runs this fabless company, with partners across InP supply, silicon foundries, and packaging, backed by a fresh, oversubscribed funding round. Sampling to customers is targeted before year-end (2026). Is this the next Philips? They are certainly making waves in the PhotonDelta ecosystem. Want to understand the business and geopolitics strategy? Watch 10 more minutes on the YouTube version here: https://lnkd.in/e3Mk_VqR

BREAKING: TSMC JUST TOLD ITS CUSTOMERS OF A PRICE HIKE FOR 2027!  Nikkei Asia reports today (21 July 2026) that TSMC is raising chipmaking prices by up to 10% starting in January 2027, covering both cutting-edge nodes (7nm and below, accounting for about 77% of Q2 revenue) and mature nodes such as 12nm to 28nm (about 23% of revenue). Customers ordering extra AI capacity beyond their original forecasts face a further 10 to 15% premium on top of the base increase, so total hikes on some advanced orders could run well past 10%.  The reasoning is straightforward. Rising costs for materials and equipment, plus the price of building new fabs in Arizona, Japan and Germany. TSMC just added another $100 billion to its Arizona investment and raised 2026 capex guidance to as much as $64 billion. CFO Wendell Huang was direct about it: overseas expansion and the 2nm ramp will continue to pressure margins.  Here is the part that matters for photonics. TSMC is no longer just a logic foundry. Its COUPE platform, which stacks photonic and electronic dies using SoIC-X and integrates through CoWoS, is TSMC's own route into co-packaged optics, and it is on track for CoWoS-based CPO integration this year. That means the same wafer capacity, advanced packaging lines and overseas fabs getting more expensive are the ones CPO for AI data centers depends on. NVIDIA's Quantum-X and Spectrum-X platforms, and the laser and materials suppliers feeding COUPE, including Coherent Corp. and Sumitomo Electric, sit downstream of this price increase.  CPO is only now moving from validation to real volume. A cost increase landing right as that transition happens will show up in every optical engine built on TSMC silicon, whether the label on the box says photonics or not. Chairman C.C. Wei's framing is worth remembering: "We earn our value and we make sure that our profit, our gross margin is enough for our long-term sustaining expansion. That's to the benefit of my customers and TSMC also."  Sources: Cheng Ting-fang, "Exclusive: TSMC to raise chipmaking prices by up to 10% from 2027," Nikkei Asia, July 21, 2026. https://lnkd.in/ezeyAx8g  TrendForce, "Silicon Photonics Race Intensifies as TSMC Targets 2026 COUPE Production, Samsung Eyes 2029 CPO Turnkey," April 1, 2026: https://lnkd.in/e6_rJvXk  TrendForce, "TSMC Reportedly Eyes Up to 15% 3nm Price Hike in 2H26, Further 5-10% Seen in 2027 Amid AI, ASIC Demand," May 27, 2026: https://lnkd.in/e-hhyKkh   Below is the TSMC Corporate video.

IBM CRASH. IS PHOTONICS OR QUANTUM THE CAUSE OR SOLUTION? On Tuesday July 14th, IBM had its worst trading day in 115 years. Shares fell 25% after the company warned that Q2 results would miss expectations: roughly $17.2B in revenue and $2.93 adjusted EPS against a $3.01 consensus, ahead of the full report on July 22.  The short answer to the headline: neither. And that is the interesting part. The cause was an AI budget war. In the final weeks of June, enterprise customers redirected capex toward supply-constrained AI infrastructure, meaning servers, storage, and memory, ahead of expected price increases driven by rising chip and memory costs. That squeezed the budgets IBM lives on: software grew only 5% against a double-digit target, consulting was flat, and infrastructure fell 7% on weak mainframe sales. CEO Arvind Krishna was blunt: "We did not adapt and move quickly enough." Quantum is not the cause. One day after the crash, IBM reaffirmed its $10B, five-year quantum investment, still targeting a fault-tolerant machine by 2029. Could photonics be part of the solution? Follow the money. The capex that abandoned IBM's software this quarter went into AI datacenter hardware, and that is precisely where optical interconnects and co-packaged optics are becoming the bottleneck technology. The spending wave that hurt IBM's quarter is the same one pulling photonics into the datacenter.  One bad quarter is not a verdict on IBM's deep-tech roadmap. But it is an early, expensive data point on where enterprise money is actually going in 2026. And if you have not watched it, remember the excellent keynote from Bert Jan Offrein at GAMA 2026. https://lnkd.in/g56CA-mz   Other Sources: https://lnkd.in/emF35V2B https://lnkd.in/evuefikH https://lnkd.in/eAwkEmtJ

MUNICH-BASED LINQUE CLAIMS ENERGY REDUCTION of 25 times! In her last contribution as Optica Director of Technology for Optics and Optical Systems, my colleague Dr Olga Raz visited Linque in Munich.  Linque is doing something the silicon photonics world has talked about for years: a large-scale programmable photonic fabric that routes optical signals on-chip, with no optical-electrical-optical conversions anywhere in the path. Their RISE64 optical circuit switch debuted at OFC Conference this year and is now in customer validation, with hyperscalers and neo-clouds both at the table.  The number that gets attention is the company's claim of 25x better energy efficiency than conventional switches. The explanation is simple: in an electrical switch, most of the power goes into SerDes, error correction, and the lasers that regenerate the signal after each conversion. Remove the conversions and that power budget largely disappears.  Two things stood out from Olga's conversation with the team. First, they are positioning as a system integrator, not a chip vendor. When asked the build-to-be-acquired question, the answer was direct: "we are more of the acquirer." Second, their win at the IC Taiwan Grand Challenge in June, one of 11 selected from 209 startups across 38 countries, opens exactly the foundry and OSAT relationships a photonics company needs to scale.  The message Linque left us with applies to the whole industry: photonics reaching product stage in the data center is not a one-company story. It takes the full semiconductor value chain, from equipment makers and fabs to OSATs, chip designers and system integrators, moving at the speed this buildout demands.

EUROPE JUST CLOSED A GAP IN ITS THIN-FILM LITHIUM NIOBATE SUPPLY CHAIN Silicon Austria Labs (SAL) Austria Labs announced today (Thursday, July 16th) that it will install the first SHINCRON Co., Ltd. INACO sputter deposition platform of its kind in Europe, at the SAL MicroFab in Villach, Austria. Until now, SAL's 200mm thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) work started at patterning and etching. This platform moves the capability upstream, into the material stack itself: in-house deposition of lithium niobate, lithium tantalate and related functional thin films, with direct control over crystallinity, stress, roughness, wafer-level uniformity and traceability.  Why this matters.  TFLN is the leading candidate material for next-generation high-speed modulators in AI data centres and for quantum photonics. And reliable access to high-quality, application-specific material stacks is one of the field's main bottlenecks. As Dr Mohssen Moridi, Senior Director at SAL, puts it: "SAL is addressing a critical bottleneck in the TFLN value chain. A European research institute now controls the full flow, from deposition through patterning, integration and industrial transfer. That reduces the continent's dependence on a small pool of external wafer suppliers and gives European TFLN device companies a local development partner." SAL is clear that the platform complements, rather than replaces, its work with established wafer and substrate suppliers.  One more thing. Silicon Austria Labs (SAL) and SHINCRON Co., Ltd. are both Optica corporate members. Operator on one side, equipment supplier on the other. That puts our membership on both sides of a strategically scarce capability. Below is an official SHINKRON video announcement.   Full announcement: https://lnkd.in/eAcCB89G

THE PRIMES HAVE STARTED BUYING THE PHOTONICS LAYER This morning Thales signed a binding agreement to acquire the Gorgé family's 35.51% stake in Optica corporate member Exail at 134 euros per share, a 44% premium, implying an enterprise value of 3.9 billion euros, with a tender offer for 100% of the company to follow.  Read that premium again. 44%. That is what a defence prime will now pay to own fibre-optic gyroscopes, inertial navigation and autonomous underwater systems rather than procure them from a supplier catalogue.  Three things I take from this deal. First, underwater is the new high ground. Seabed cables and pipelines have become critical infrastructure, and protecting them in GPS-denied environments is a photonics problem. Thales plus Exail creates a European champion in exactly that space.  Second, navigation-grade photonics just got repriced. Every independent European company in inertial sensing, sonar and quantum navigation is worth more today than it was on Friday, and more strategic too. I would not be surprised to see Kongsberg or Saab respond.  Third, the deal will not close until late 2027, but the signal is immediate: photonics is no longer a component decision in defence. It is a balance sheet decision.  Exail is an Optica corporate member, and they will host the Optica Aerospace & Defense Summit on 9 and 10 December. The timing could hardly be better. Come and see the technology the primes are paying premiums for. More info here. https://lnkd.in/e3Mj_ap8 Source: Thales press release, 6 July 2026: https://lnkd.in/ehM6RBaE

META IS SELLING THE THING IT SAID IT NEEDED MORE OF Meta just admitted it has more AI compute than it knows what to do with. That single fact explains more about the state of the AI trade than any earnings call this quarter. On July 1, Bloomberg reported Meta is building a new unit, internally called Meta Compute, to sell its spare GPU capacity to outside customers. Two models are reportedly on the table: hosted access to Meta's own AI (including its new Muse Spark model, the AWS Bedrock playbook) or straight compute rental, the CoreWeave playbook. The unit is said to be run by infrastructure chief Santosh Janardhan, Superintelligence Labs lead Daniel Gross, and president Dina Powell McCormick.  The market's reaction tells you where the real anxiety sits. Meta stock jumped more than 10% that day, its best single session in five months, after a year in which the stock had fallen close to 15% and lagged the S&P 500. CoreWeave fell 10.8%. Nebius fell 12.4%. Meta has committed roughly $48 billion to renting GPUs from those two companies (a $21 billion CoreWeave deal, expanded in April, plus up to $27 billion with Nebius) because its own buildout couldn't keep pace with demand. If Meta suddenly has capacity to spare, either its infrastructure finally caught up, or the demand it built for never fully showed up.  That's the case Ed Zitron made to Ed Elson on this week's Prof G Markets. Meta still doesn't break out Meta AI or Llama revenue the way Google or OpenAI report their AI businesses, so reselling the leftover GPUs is the clearest return Meta can point to on its spending so far. Zitron's broader argument, laid out in his newsletter, is that most of the AI capex boom is really being justified by two customers, Anthropic and OpenAI, who account for the bulk of the industry's disclosed AI revenue. Strip them out and the rest of the "AI economy" thins out fast. As he posted on X the day the story broke: "We'll know we will hit the top when Meta sells compute to Anthropic."  Worth a counterpoint. SpaceX did something similar with xAI's Colossus 1 site, leasing capacity to Anthropic for roughly $1.25 billion a month and to Google for about $920 million a month, a pair of deals Bloomberg Intelligence estimates could be worth over $50 billion by 2028. Reselling spare capacity isn't automatically a distress signal. It can just as easily be a company admitting it over-built for a demand curve that hasn't arrived on schedule, and doing something rational with the surplus while it waits.  Which gets to the actual question worth asking: who is monetizing AI right now, in dollars, not narrative? NVIDIA's data center revenue hit $75.2 billion last quarter, up 92% year over year, at a 75% gross margin. Everyone downstream of Nvidia, Meta included, is still spending to find out if the bet pays off. https://lnkd.in/eSS7hccm

THE TWO-YEAR CLOCK ON CO-PACKAGED OPTICS: A NINE-MONTH SCOREBOARD Last September, at ECOC in Copenhagen, I chaired a fireside session on the Market Focus stage that I still think about. We put three companies up there, each holding one piece of a puzzle nobody has solved yet: how do you get co-packaged optics into data centers in two years, not five.  Coherent Corp. brought socket CPO. POET Technologies argued for putting the laser outside the package, easier thermal management, a path to pluggables. Photon Bridge argued for putting it inside, better manufacturability and cost, but harder engineering. The audience in the room was split roughly down the middle. Nobody, including the panelists, had the full answer.  Nine months on, with ECOC Exhibition and the Optica Global Photonics Economic Forum both landing in Malaga the same week this September, it's worth checking the scoreboard.  Coherent Corp.: In March, Nvidia took a $2 billion equity stake in Coherent and signed a multi-year, multi-billion-dollar purchase commitment covering CPO and optical circuit switching. Coherent used the moment to raise its CPO addressable market estimate to $15 billion. That is a hyperscaler underwriting the socket CPO bet with capital, not just intent.   POET Technologies: At OFC in March, POET showed Blazar, its hybrid laser for CPO and chip-to-chip optical links, alongside Starlight, a next-gen external light source engine. It also has a joint development agreement with Lumilens that includes a $50 million initial purchase order, and a partnership with Sivers Semiconductors targeting customer prototypes in the first half of 2026 and production readiness by year-end.   Photon Bridge: Since unveiling its integrated tunable laser PIC last October, it has moved fast on the supply side. OE Solutions is using Photonbridge's laser engine inside its next-gen ITLA modules, with sampling due Q2 2026. It has also partnered with CPFC on multi-wavelength InP laser wafers and with PhiX on DWDM external laser sources for CPO and dense optical interconnects. (  None of this settles the outside-versus-inside debate. What it shows is that all three paths attracted real capital and real customers in the space of two quarters. That is exactly the pace I said the industry would need - not necessarily the right form factor or price point, but a real product, tested and working inside a live data center.  That is the bar. Malaga in September is where we find out how close we got. https://lnkd.in/eP9_DVDt And join us for the Optica Global Photonics Economic Forum. https://lnkd.in/eiyZTQEV

CANADA'S PHOTONICS MOMENT: WHY THE CPFC SPINOFF CHANGES EVERYTHING FOR WESTERN SEMICONDUCTOR SUPPLY CHAINS  The Canadian Photonics Fabrication Center is being spun off from the National Research Council Canada / Conseil national de recherches Canada as a private, independent foundry. That is not a minor administrative change. It is the moment a fragmented set of assets becomes a coherent supply chain.  I chatted with Paul Slaby, Managing Director of the Canadian Semiconductor Council, to understand what this actually means -- for the industry, for AI infrastructure, and for Western supply chain sovereignty.  A few things that struck me from this conversation: CPFC is now the only pure-play III-V foundry in North America. One of very few in the world. And it sits at the center of a cluster most people outside the industry have not yet mapped: Ciena's global R&D is managed out of Ottawa. Nokia's Optical Division is headquartered there. Jabil's co-packaged optics R&D facility is in Kanata. Inphos, building devices directly from CPFC output, is run by Joe Costello. This is not a collection of companies. It is a supply chain.  Paul is direct about what scaling this requires. Current CPFC assets are around half a billion dollars. He puts the investment needed to make this fully competitive -- from wafer capacity expansion to the R&D required to hit the yields AI applications demand from high-power indium phosphide -- somewhere between $5 billion and $10 billion. Large numbers. But not TSMC-scale silicon numbers. And the demand justification is there: the compound semiconductor market is forecast to grow from roughly $5 billion today to $30 billion within five years, driven almost entirely by AI data center optical interconnect requirements.  We also get into critical minerals. Canada holds the second-largest known reserves of the rare earth materials that III-V manufacturing depends on. The challenge is not what is in the ground. It is the decade-long timeline to bring a mine from discovery to production-ready output. That conversation on substrate supply and minerals sovereignty is one of the more frank exchanges in the interview.  If you are building partnerships in Canada, evaluating the Ottawa photonics cluster, or trying to understand where Western compound semiconductor capacity is actually coming from -- this is 15 minutes worth your time.

Difficult to understand the timing of this move by Optica corporate member Leidos when quantum is becoming ever more important to the defense and financial security sector. Given the news, I expect others will quickly secure this team of talent, which ranks among the smartest in the world. I wish everyone success in the next assignment. Please keep in touch.

When I started at Leidos in late 2020, I persuaded leadership to invest in quantum — from a whiteboard idea to a weapons-grade team doing real science. A $25M DARPA quantum-networking program. Single-photon detectors measured to ~1%. Fault-tolerant algorithms for problems classical machines can't touch. Photonic devices taken from design to fabricated silicon.  Recently, Leidos wound that team down.  I don't say this lightly: this is one of the most talented groups I've ever had the privilege to build alongside. "Smart" doesn't cover it. These are the people you want in the room when the problem looks impossible — and they're now available.  So I'm doing the most useful thing I can: putting my name behind every one of them.  If you lead a team in quantum, photonics, sensing, or advanced computing — or you just hire brilliant scientists and engineers — read this list and reach out. I'll make the intro personally.  In no particular order:  Dr. Nam N. — quantum algorithms. PI on DARPA Quantum Benchmarking; makes quantum computing useful for hard chemistry and linear systems. 15+ papers, multiple patents. Shreya K. Patel, PhD — nanophotonics, magnetics, and device fabrication. All-optical computing and neural networks; turns light into computation — and wins the grants to fund it. Dr. Matthew Wampler — theoretical quantum physicist. Designs the control and error-correction protocols that make qubits behave. Physical Review X / PRB author. Ashlee Henig, PhD, PMP — the rare scientist-program-manager: ran a $25M DARPA program and brought it from Red to Green, on schedule and on budget. Dr. Benjamin McDowell — quantum materials and integrated-photonic device design; lead contributor on a winning ARPA-I quantum-sensing submission. Chamithri Adikarige — experimental physicist. Single-photon detectors and integrated photonics; built wafer-level test platforms (formerly PsiQuantum). Zach Hainsel — optical and quantum sensing across DARPA programs; 20+ DoD field tests, co-PI experience. Pranav Kalinadhabhotla — quantum sensing: NV-diamond magnetometry, QKD, and a DoD SBIR award. Ranjani Sundaram — quantum networking and distributed quantum computing; algorithms that cut comms cost and latency 50–80%. (NVIDIA CUDA-Q intern; Stony Brook PhD.) Eli Briskin — photonics and optical engineering; experimental automation and photonic-IC test at MIT Lincoln Lab. Exceptional early-career talent. Grace Neil — rising quantum-optics researcher; laser and atomic (rubidium) spectroscopy. An undergraduate researcher with real momentum.  Several of them hold active security clearances.  The best thing you can do in ten seconds: share this, or tag someone hiring.   And to this team — it was an honor. You built something real, and the work doesn't disappear because an org chart changed. REINDUSTRIALISATION IS NOT about turning the clock back. In a tense geopolitical moment, the easy instinct is to protect legacy industries. The leaders actually building capacity have rejected that. You do not reshore by fighting for an industry someone else has dominated for generations. You reshore by owning the one that comes next.  Jensen Huang said it plainly at Coherent Corp.'s groundbreaking in Sherman, Texas: you cannot compete with someone doing what they have done for 50 or 100 years, so you look for a transition, a phase shift, a new industry where new skills, new factories and new companies can exist. The plants that were unnecessary or insufficient before, for chips, packaging and photonics, are precisely the ones AI now demands.  We have seen this pattern before. Automotive lighting did not defend the headlight. As ams OSRAM CEO Aldo Kamper has described, the value moved to sensing, visualisation and new semiconductor content. Lead, never follow. Photonics and semiconductor leaders understood this early, and they understood something else: in challenging geopolitics, a strong regional strategy is not optional. Jim Anderson is scaling 6-inch indium phosphide at Coherent in Texas, the building blocks of AI infrastructure, backed by CHIPS Act support and a NVIDIA relationship spanning more than two decades. Edwin Roks, now CEO of TTM Technologies, carries the same conviction from semiconductors and advanced electronics. What unites them is not nostalgia. It is a clear plan built for the technology arriving, not the one leaving.  The hard question is regional. North America, Europe and Asia each need a different route to a defensible position, and no single playbook covers all three. That is the agenda at the Global Photonics Economic Forum in Málaga on 24 and 25 September. If you want to influence the global photonics conversation, join a boardroom-level group of CEOs, CTOs, investors and policymakers working through the regional strategies that decide who grows in a volatile landscape. check the lineup and be in the room. https://lnkd.in/gBuRVF89

EIGHT COMPANIES, ONE MESSAGE: QUANTUM IS READY TO SCALE Time for some reporting from the exhibition floor in Glasgow, where Optica Quantum 2.0 and the Quantum Industry Summit were co-located for the first time (15 to 18 June, Scottish Event Campus). The throughline: quantum is moving from the lab to the production line.  What stood out: VIAVI Solutions (Ines Brunn): the MAP platform to test photonics and emulate quantum signals over a network before deployment. Proximion, nkiw part of Hexatronic (Husain Imam): chirped fibre Bragg gratings extending QKD reach and characterising entangled sources. AeroDIODE (Paul Renard ): custom narrow-linewidth laser diodes with low-noise electronics. Noisy Labs (Dr. Axel Schönbeck): commercial squeezed-light lasers for quantum sensing and computing. nVent SCHROFF (Udo Weiss): modular chassis, cooling and infrastructure to take systems from bench to volume. G&H (Gooch & Housego) (Gary Sinclair): optical coatings and fibre-coupled modulators with high extinction ratios. SmarAct Group (Ezzeddine Ben Ammar): compact positioning for 4 K, ultra-high vacuum and non-magnetic environments. Exail (Clément RAIGNER): CV-QKD from off-the-shelf components, plus Muquans quantum gravimeters.  Three takeaways: scale fast, deploy now with reliable off-the-shelf parts, and watch quantum converge with AI, defence and space. The capital is following too, with Quantinuum's recent Nasdaq listing.  Thanks to all who shared their time. https://lnkd.in/e3Mj_ap8 Hope to see you in Paris in December.

WHAT DID QUIX JUST MAKE OBSOLETE? EVERY PHOTONIC QUANTUM COMPUTER THAT CAME BEFORE IT. That was the claim from Jelmer Renema, co-founder and Chief Scientist of QuiX Quantum, at last week's Optica Quantum Industry Summit in Glasgow, collocated this year with Quantum 2.0. On the exhibition floor, he announced Carina, which QuiX is calling the first universal photonic quantum computer deployed to a customer site.  Universal is the operative word. It means the machine can execute any quantum algorithm, including the famous ones like Shor's and Grover's that require arbitrary quantum computation rather than a narrow set of operations tailored to the hardware. Until now, most of the field has been building algorithms to fit the computer. QuiX is arguing the order should be reversed.  The technology that makes this possible, according to Renema, is feed-forward. A photonic computer works by sending photons through an optical system. If you can switch that optical system in real time based on what some of the photons are doing, you can steer it toward arbitrary quantum computations. Doing that at the speed of light demands a tight combination of fast electronics, high-quality detectors, precise optics, and nonlinear optics, all integrated into a single package. Bringing those components together in a deployed device is what QuiX says makes prior delivered systems obsolete.  Carina is NOT a lab demonstration. Renema said it is in production, commercially available, and purchasable today, with cloud access already live through QuiX's website. The design choices are aimed squarely at the AI data center buildout: room temperature operation, modular architecture, and scaling through fiber connections, which positions photonic quantum hardware to ride the same infrastructure wave that is already pulling photonics into data centers.  On scaling, Renema was direct about the open problems the Summit kept returning to: losses in fiber to chip coupling and modulator efficiency. His case for optimism is that the hardware requirements can be relaxed through software. He pointed to the long-run reduction in qubits needed to run Shor's algorithm, and to a photonics-tailored error correction strategy QuiX says outperforms conventional quantum error correction by a factor of four, lowering the cost of the machine.  The roadmap from here is utility scale: large systems of interlinked, fiber-coupled optical racks on a data center floor, stabilized and controlled by a classical computer alongside them, running real industry workloads. With Xanadu, PsiQuantum, IonQ and others in the room, Renema's summary of the week was blunt. The race is on, and quantum utility is now on the horizon.
Excellent summary. Worth your time reading.

Greater Glasgow was recently ranked the fifth most important quantum cluster in the world, and first globally for its role as a sector hub. That’s an incredible achievement for the UK, and a massive nod to Scotland's deep-tech ecosystem.  This week I’ve been following discussions at Optica's Quantum 2.0 Conference in Glasgow (15–18 June) where one of the themes has been the race to translate raw R&D into practical, commercial applications.  Jose Pozo, CTO of Optica, captured the challenge. He pointed out that quantum is currently moving at two separate speeds: academic research (measured in qubits) and industry reality (focused on scalable products). At the moment, they’re not aligned. While the science is forging ahead, the market is lagging in building the next-generation hardware needed to protect our critical infrastructure.  The timeline is tight. According to some predictions, quantum hardware capable of cracking current security algorithms could exist within three years. Andrew Shields of Toshiba Europe UK thought a ten-year horizon was more realistic, but there’s great urgency nonetheless. Alongside resilient hardware, we also need new data protocols and certification standards.  It was very positive to hear about the local expertise that’s helping to address this challenge. Scottish space company Craft Prospect is already working on using satellites to facilitate Quantum Key Distribution from space – a vital step in scaling these networks from regional to global models.  For me, the key message was Jose’s rallying cry at the end of his opening presentation: ‘Connect the bench to the market’. His advice for closing the divide was direct:  ⚛️ To the researchers: Bring the hard problems and the honest data – not the press release. 🏢 To industry: Bring the real-world use cases, the capital, and the procurement appetite to make deployment a reality.  In a sector changing this quickly, clear communication is going to play a key role. Alongside the researchers and companies developing the tech, we need to educate the wider community about what quantum will offer – the great benefits, but also the threats to our critical infrastructure and daily lives if research and industry don’t align.  This ties in perfectly with the work being done by QURECA, which was recently highlighted at the Quantum ARC event in Glasgow. That marked the launch of a new report titled ‘Inside Scotland’s Quantum Ecosystem’ – yet another reminder of the country’s key role in the quantum race.

TWO CORPORATE Optica MEMBERS UNITE: We are glad to see two Optica corporate members, PhotonPath and XIVER, join forces. PhotonPath has announced a strategic collaboration with XIVER to scale production of Oxyn, its proprietary integrated photonics platform for telecom and datacom applications.  The fit is clear. PhotonPath brings the platform and the integrated photonics design expertise. XIVER brings 200 mm wafer infrastructure and photonics-ready manufacturing know-how. Together, they are set up to increase production capacity, shorten lead times, and deliver consistent, repeatable quality for high-performance photonic devices, which is exactly what high-volume markets demand.  As Emanuele Guglielmi, CTO and co-founder of PhotonPath, puts it: "It also lays the foundation for our next milestones, including the integration of photodiodes directly into the platform. Customers can be confident that our solutions are built on a technology designed for both performance and scalability." Wil Van de Wiel, CEO of XIVER, added: "Oxyn not only exceeds market requirements for performance and reliability, but its scalability onto XIVER's 200 mm wafer line also makes it a highly compelling solution for high-volume photonic devices serving AI-driven data infrastructure and next-generation optical systems."  This is the kind of partnership that moves integrated photonics from promising technology to manufacturable product, and it matters for the AI data infrastructure and optical systems being built right now.  We will follow up soon with video interviews from both teams. Stay tuned.

OpenAI SPENT $5.73 BILLION ON MARKETING LAST YEAR - HOW IS THIS POSSIBLE? OpenAI’s 2025 financials leaked this week, first published by Ed Zitron and independently verified by the Financial Times. Most people focused on the $20.9B operating loss against $13.1B in revenue. The line that Ed keeps coming back to is sales and marketing: $5.73B, up roughly fivefold from $1.11B a year earlier.  How is that possible for a company to lose money on nearly every dollar it brings in?  Because the price of OpenAI is not set by its fundamentals. It is set by the marginal buyer. The last investor into the last round marks the entire cap table, and as long as one more buyer will pay a higher number, the story holds. That is how you fund a $5.73B marketing budget while the operating line bleeds.  I am not predicting a crash. I am pointing at the part of the stack that already pays for itself. The model layer raises money on a story. The optics and silicon underneath it sell on cost per bit and power per rack. Every token that the company ever serves has to travel across glass to get there.  When NVIDIA launched its Spectrum-X and Quantum-X co-packaged optics switches at GTC 2025, the named partners were Coherent Corp., Corning Incorporated, Lumentum, TSMC and Foxconn. Real buyers, real volume, real cost-down curves. No round of funding is required to justify the demand.  The marginal buyer sets the valuation. The photon sets the limit. What are you watching in the leaked numbers? Anyone concerned?  Sign up for Prof. G Markets (on YouTube) for excellent free insights. Sources OpenAI 2025 financials (Fortune, citing Ed Zitron and Financial Times verification): fortune.com Ed Zitron, original report: wheresyoured.at NVIDIA Spectrum-X / Quantum-X co-packaged optics and partners (GTC 2025): nvidianews.nvidia.com

M & A UPDATE TTM Technologies moves into Europe with two PCB acquisitions. Optica corporate member TTM Technologies (NASDAQ: TTMI) announced on 17 June the planned acquisition of two European printed circuit board specialists, Swiss Technology Group AG (STG) of Zurich and ILFA GmbH of Hannover. Both are all-cash transactions, subject to regulatory approval, with closing expected in Q3 2026 and described by TTM as immediately but modestly accretive. STG, formed in 2023 from the merger of GS Swiss PCB and the Hofstetter Group, specialises in highly miniaturised microcircuits for implantable neurostimulators, leadless pacemakers, cochlear implants and surgical robotics, with roughly $50 million of fiscal 2025 revenue and around 200 staff.  ILFA, a 45-year-old Hannover manufacturer with about 190 employees, builds complex PCBs for aerospace and defence, medical and industrial markets, including electro-optical boards, embedded component integration and fluid channel control, and holds 12 patent families. Citi is advising TTM on the STG transaction and Piper Sandler is advising STG.  What this means: The strategic value is in photonics-adjacent packaging. Electro-optical PCBs, waveguide embedding and miniaturised substrates for implantable photonic devices are exactly where optical functionality has to survive aggressive packaging constraints in medical and aerospace systems. "Chips don't float," TTM President and CEO Edwin Roks said in a recent Optica interview, pointing to the interconnect layer as the place where photonic and electronic systems finally have to coexist, with no tolerance margin in implantables or aerospace sensors. For European photonics, the deal is double-edged: it brings TTM investment and long-cycle demand into the region, while again moving specialist European manufacturing IP under US ownership. TTM expects to take investor questions on both deals during its Q2 earnings call, currently projected for early August 2026.  The video below was originally posted on May 28th  https://lnkd.in/eStAxz-U

This is an important move. On 16 June, Coherent (NYSE: COHR) signed a letter of intent for up to $50 million in direct CHIPS and Science Act funding from the U.S. Department of Commerce to expand its 6-inch indium phosphide manufacturing platform in Sherman, Texas, described as the world's first and largest volume-production 6-inch InP line. The project will double production space and quadruple wafer capacity, create more than 1,000 jobs (over 550 of them direct manufacturing and engineering roles), and builds on roughly $20 million of prior state and local support. CEO Jim Anderson framed InP photonic devices as building blocks for AI infrastructure, and the announcement coincided with a groundbreaking ceremony attended by NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, deepening a supplier relationship that spans more than two decades. For European photonics, the competitive read is direct: NVIDIA is locking in InP supply across favoured partners, and European device makers without secured substrate and capacity risk being designed out of the hyperscale optical roadmap. At least that's how it looks at the moment. Comments welcome.

AI infrastructure runs on more than compute. It runs on light.  Today in Sherman, Texas, our CEO Jensen Huang joined Coherent Corp. CEO Jim Anderson and local leaders to mark the expansion of U.S. manufacturing capacity for advanced photonics.  The technology is behind the lasers and optical links that move data across modern AI systems, strengthening the American manufacturing base behind the AI era.

WILL QUANTUM SCALE OR FAIL? That was the question running through my opening remarks on Tuesday at the Optica Quantum 2.0 and Quantum Industry Summit in Glasgow this week, before an audience of 750 and a record 61 exhibition booths. Here is what I wanted everyone in the room to take away. Watch the video below to see the clip. Quantum runs on two clocks. One is R&D, measured in qubits and bit error rate. The other is commercial, measured in investment, manufacturing capacity and speed to market. These two clocks report to different stakeholders, and the whole point of bringing the research community and the industrial community into the same building is to make them tick together.  Why now? Because the money has changed character. Three of the world's largest economies are treating quantum as critical infrastructure with taxpayer money behind it: roughly 2bn USD in the US alongside IBM's 11bn commitment to manufacture quantum at home, 11bn in the EU Quantum Act over the next five years, and 2bn GBP in the UK. On the private side, Quantinuum is heading to an IPO near 1.7bn USD and Oxford Quantum Circuits (OQC) raised 260m GBP, the largest round ever for a European quantum company.  The technical progress is real, too. We are seeing error correction progress quickly, including demonstrations of correcting 40 logical qubits out of 90. But the most important signal came from two companies I spoke to before the event. QuiX Quantum in Enschede is building photonic quantum chips designed so the hardware naturally fits the customer's algorithm, particularly for quantum chemistry. Jelmer Renema, CSO: people are lining up at the door. Imran Khan, Managing Director of German CVQKD company, KEEQuant, building integrated photonics for quantum key distribution, put it more bluntly: the threat is real now, telecom operators need these chips in volume today, and we cannot wait for the threat to materialise. They have already run a 900km carrier-grade link between Berlin and Bonn, delivered as keys-as-a-service.  That is the shift. A few years ago, quantum capital was blue-sky money. Today, every investor in the room is asking the same question: if I put money in, will you be in production in three years?  The technology is ready. The capital is ready. What remains is execution. We either scale or we fail.  By Thursday, if you were in Glasgow, do not tell me the summit was interesting. Tell me you connected with three real leads, and that at least one came from the other side of the aisle. Comments and suggestions welcome below. If you are in Glasgow, please say hello and connect with others (including me).   Next stop, Copenhagen, 7 to 10 June 2027. Onward!

ASML KEYNOTE: CALL FOR OPEN COLLABORATION IN PHOTONICS. At the Optica GAMA Meeting 2026 in Brussels last week (June 3rd), Henk Niesing , VP and Head of Technology EU/US at ASML delivered a special opening keynote on Day 2. The session opened with a direct announcement: next year's GAMA meeting will be hosted by ASML at its campus in Veldhoven, The Netherlands 12 – 13 May 2027. Mark that date in the calendar. You can watch the keynote presentation here: https://lnkd.in/eVTmiiT4  Niesing set the context quickly. AI is driving demand across the entire wafer production spectrum -- not just at the advanced EUV nodes that dominate headlines, but across the mainstream logic, analogue, power, photonics and sensor markets that represent the majority of wafers produced globally. That distinction matters for anyone in the room building components for the photonics supply chain.  The first half covered Moore's Law and packaging. Niesing argued that the scaling roadmap remains valid through 2042 but now requires a 16x improvement in compute per two-year cycle -- impossible on 2D scaling alone. The answer is heterogeneous 3D integration, and package sizes are growing as fast as devices are shrinking. ASML's response is a new lithography system with a 4x larger exposure field (52 by 68mm), designed specifically for the interposer layer where logic, memory and photonics converge. A full-wafer exposure capability is in development.  The second half addressed photonics patterning directly. Niesing walked through the challenges ASML hears from photonics customers: substrate diversity (InP still on 4-inch wafers), large topography of V-grooves, CD uniformity, pitch stability, and line-edge roughness. He presented ASML's holistic lithography approach -- combining scanner optimisation, computational lithography (OPC), and e-beam metrology -- with specific results: He identified resist selection and mask optimisation as two further levers the industry is underusing.  ASML's open collaboration request: any fab that measures both lithography parameters and optical propagation losses in the same process flow is a potential partner. That link is currently missing.  The Q&A included an exchange with Liron Gantz of NVIDIA, who noted that even indirect suppliers -- components going into the photonics supply chain -- should engage directly with ASML on the technology roadmap rather than waiting for requirements to filter down. Niesing agreed and called it exactly the kind of input ASML needs years in advance. Grab a coffee!

IN PHOTONICS, EUROPE STILL LEADS. THE OPEN QUESTION IS WHETHER WE KEEP IT. I caught up with Lutz Aschke, President of Photonics21, at the annual meeting in Brussels this week. Lutz is also a board member of Light Conversion in Lithuania and works with Marvel Fusion in Germany, so he speaks from inside the companies, not only from the policy room.  What is working in Europe: Europe leads the world in photonics, and 80% of that leadership has nothing to do with semiconductors. Ultrashort-pulse lasers, laser cutting, and lens making. This is core to our strategic autonomy, and it is easy to forget when every headline is about chips. Chips Joint Undertaking matters, but photonics is far bigger than its semiconductor slice. The base is strong too: a deep university landscape, a Photonics21 community of 4,500 members, mostly SMEs, and a venture capital environment that has genuinely improved.  What is too slow: Scaling. In Lutz's words, "we are good at inventing things and building prototypes, we struggle with scaling." The gap is specific. Companies can raise early money, and once they pass roughly 50 million Euro in revenue a later-stage ecosystem funds them. In between, there is a hole. The European Commission sees it. But the problem is far from being solved. And the real constraint is risk appetite, not cost. Automation has reduced the role of labour costs. What holds us back is the risk tolerance of industry, society, and policymakers.  Two examples he keeps returning to: Lithuania got it right. Vilnius matched R&D with manufacturing and valorisation in the same country, built on a strong talent pipeline. Light Conversion grew into the world's most important femtosecond laser maker from that base. Germany is where the next bet sits. Marvel Fusion is now the leader in laser fusion worldwide. Lutz expects first-of-a-kind and early commercial reactors in the 2030s, a complement to renewables and a route to energy independence, since Europe already has the resources fusion needs.  His warning is blunt. We let semiconductor manufacturing disappear, and regaining that autonomy is now enormously expensive. In photonics, we still lead, so holding the line costs far less than rebuilding it would. "Otherwise it costs us 10 times more to reestablish that, if it is possible at all." There was a sense of urgency in the room this year. We are still ahead. Now we have to act like it. Agree? Comment below.

ELEPHANTS IN THE ROOM? We continue our deep-tech coverage of last week's Optica GAMA meeting in Brussels, with keynotes now on YouTube from PhotonHub https://lnkd.in/eyZubp7u, IBM https://lnkd.in/g56CA-mz and Intel Foundry https://lnkd.in/emWpCiUN. These were honest engineering discussions about the real problems photonics companies face when they try to scale, not marketing spin. At recent meetings, also in Brussels, several of us have kept returning to one huge contradiction. We talk about European leadership, yet our substrates come largely from Asia, equipment suppliers often serve non-European customers first, and many European fabless PIC companies manufacture their chips outside Europe. If that is the supply chain we actually have, it is fair to ask whether funding more pilot lines for already mature technologies is the right move? Europe does not have a pilot-line problem. It has a commercialisation and industrial-scale manufacturing problem.  So on the morning of Day 2 at GAMA, before the ASML keynote, I walked the room to hear what people were actually saying. Ignazio Piacentini (IP Consulting), who works on assembly and test rather than devices, put it plainly. The story from day one was high-volume manufacturing, and it all turns on co-packaged optics and massive optical interconnect. The pull is coming from semiconductors, which are absorbing photonics as an enabling technology, and that is forcing the equipment makers to the next level. As he put it, ficonTEC is not moving from 1.0 to 2.0. It is heading to 3.0, and the rest of the equipment base will have to follow.  Then Andrea Rocchetto (EPHOS) raised the point that stayed with me. Two weeks ago the The Royal Society in London ran a photonics war game with three blocks. The West played IP as its strength. China played mass-scale volume manufacturing. South East Asia played its card in advanced packaging. The result was a clear win for China. Andrea would like to run the same game with an audience like this one, which is far more technical and informed. So would I. The rules and the final report are published on the Royal Society site so we hope to investigate further.   That is the elephant. We have the IP. We are short on the volume. The encouraging part is who was in the room. Bert Jan Offrein (IBM), Bernard Lee (SENKO), Sébastien Libon (ESA), Alexander Kessel (Intego), Lars Benesch (Photonics Supply and Services Partner) and others. And when it comes to semiconductor manufacturing at scale, Europe does have a world leader, which is why we opened Day 2 with a keynote and an announcement from Henk Niesing, head of technology at ASML. More coverage to follow.  Tell me where you think Europe should place its bets over the next five years.

INTEL KEYNOTE: Advanced packaging as the critical enabler. GRAB A COFFEE AND DON'T MISS THIS! Feeds from last week's GAMA meeting in Brussels have started all kinds of conversations. I am sure this will be another one. Watch this presentation on YouTube: https://lnkd.in/emWpCiUN  Nick Psaila, formerly of OptoScribe and now leading co-packaged optics (CPO) development at Intel Foundry, delivered an excellent keynote at Optica Gamma Brussels 2026. His talk cut straight to the core problem: AI systems are no longer compute-limited, they are IO-limited, and copper interconnects are running out of road.  Psaila laid out why CPO is not a future technology but a present-tense supply chain emergency. With at least five one-gigawatt data centres expected online this year, and projections of over 100 million 1600G-equivalent optical links per facility, he warned the entire photonics supply chain, from PIC fabs to FAU manufacturers to assembly and metrology equipment providers, that volume investment is required now.  The talk then moved into advanced packaging as the critical enabler. Psaila argued that CPO is as much a packaging challenge as a photonics one, and that the industry needs to abandon artisan assembly processes in favour of wafer-level, high-throughput, JEDEC-qualified manufacturing compatible with heterogeneous integration and 3D stacking. He outlined Intel's packaging spectrum from EMIB to Foveros, and introduced their work on glass core substrates, including the first demonstration of a 24-layer glass core with embedded EMIB presented at ECTC the previous week. Glass core, he argued, solves the CTE mismatch problem that increasingly limits organic substrates at large panel sizes, and opens a path to integrating 3D waveguides directly into the substrate for in-package optical interconnects.  He closed with a long-range vision: panel-scale compute with an embedded optical connectivity matrix that routes signals from die-to-die to the external network within a single architecture.  The Q&A that followed was one of the more direct exchanges of the day. It has been edited out of this version. Representatives from Opsydia, Ephos, and FEMTOPRINT SA each weighed in on their own approaches to direct laser writing for in-package waveguides, with Rolando Ferrini from FEMTOPRINT SA making the pointed argument that the industry cannot rely on a single supplier at this scale and that cross-licensing between European competitors may ultimately serve the market better than winner-takes-all competition. A voice-of-the-customer moment from Peter Kirkegaard cut through the noise: demand will be high enough that there is work for everyone. Further questions addressed polymer microoptics and their reliability constraints under reflow and TCB conditions, as well as how companies like Demcon Photonics are working to bring direct laser writing processes into foundry-compatible, high-volume manufacturing. For me, one of the best presentations in a long time. Agree?
Useful to know. Wonder what effect it will have on photonics in the Netherlands.

While  hashtag #ASML's share price hit a new record, the Netherlands has more crucial technology to offer than litho tools from Veldhoven.  That is why the Dutch cabinet is planning to better shield vital technology from foreign takeovers. AI, biotechnology, and the development of advanced materials and sensor technology will soon be added to the list of 'sensitive' applications, to prevent them from falling into the wrong hands and being used for espionage or sabotage.  On Monday, Minister of Economic Affairs and Climate Heleen Herbert (CDA) announced that she is adding six new technologies to the Security Screening of Investments, Mergers and Acquisitions Act ( hashtag #Vifo). This act has protected quantum computers, photonics, semiconductors, chip equipment and other technologies with potential military applications since 2023. Other categories to be added to the 'sensitive' Vifo list from 2027: nanotechnology, genetic modification, location technology and nuclear applications for medical use.  The Vifo Act can be applied retroactively to 2020. That date does not reach quite far enough to reverse the Chinese takeover of chipmaker  hashtag #Nexperia (2019). When the Ministry of Economic Affairs decided in September 2025 to intervene at Nexperia — because the company was seeking to transfer vital knowledge and expertise to China — caretaker minister Vincent Karremans did so on the basis of a different law. He fell back on the obscure Availability of Goods Act, conceived in the 1950s but never previously used. The law prevents major changes within a company from being implemented without the minister's approval.  The Netherlands is becoming increasingly aware that foreign takeovers can cause problems, as shown recently when State Secretary Willemijn Aerdts (Digital Economy and Sovereignty, D66) blocked the proposed takeover of  hashtag #DigiD operator  hashtag #Solvinity by American firm  hashtag #Kyndryl.  Full article in NRC in comments 👇

EUROPE's PHOTONIC SCALEUP SCENE: WHAT IS WORKING, WHAT IS NOT, AND WHO SHOULD BE ALLOWED TO FAIL. It is an important week of discussion for Photonics21. Last week, an audience of 110 looked at one of the most important schemes running in Photonics21. Hugo Thienpont, Director of VUB B-PHOT Brussels Photonics and coordinator of PhotonHub Europe, maps the current state of the European photonics startup and scaleup ecosystem—where genuine momentum is building and where structural gaps are holding companies back. He was speaking on June 2nd to open the Optica Global Advanced Manufacturing Alliance meeting. Hugo Thienpont responds to multiple questions throughout the keynote. You can watch the complete presentation + Q & A here. https://lnkd.in/eyZubp7u (48 minutes).   The 20-min opening presentation sparked a pointed discussion about the European Commission's role: are current instruments actually accelerating commercialisation, or are they propping up companies that should not exist?  Audience reactions included:  2. Yannick Paillard - Discusses wafer-level manufacturing 3. Ignazio Piacentini - Asks questions about photonics visibility at the EU level 4. Benoît d'Humières - Comments on photonics industry support and public-private partnerships1 5. Marijke Keters - discusses demand-side issues. 6. Lars Benesch - asks about support 7. Darren Berns - From IDEX, discusses bridging the gap between European innovation and market scaling.  8. Liron Gantz addresses photonics visibility and foundry needs 9. Reinhard Voelkel- Discusses product-market fit and the need for stricter company evaluation 10. Philippe Bolle - Comments on industrialisation challenges and European VC funding issues  The sharpest intervention came from Reinhard Voelkel, who argued that failures should be allowed to die. His case: Europe does not have a zombie scale-up problem due to a lack of funding. It has a serial entrepreneur problem. Capital recycled through failed ventures, and the people who ran them, is how ecosystems build compounding know-how. Keeping the walking dead on life support blocks both. It is an uncomfortable argument -- but a necessary one for an industry that needs to move rapidly from world-class research to world-class companies. What do you think: should EU instruments set harder gates on product readiness before a scaleup qualifies for support? And do you agree that we should do more at the national level in Europe.

LEARN FROM Lumentum: BORROWED FROM SEMICONDUCTORS, BUILT FOR PHOTONICS: THE PACKAGING MOVE AI CONNECTIVITY NEEDS At Optica GAMA last week (June 2-3 2026), Matt Guzy, Advanced Packaging lead at Lumentum, did something rare: he showed us a working prototype and explained exactly how it was built. Watch his presentation here https://lnkd.in/gR9EyFYu.   Matt came to Lumentum after 21 years at Intel, where he worked on some of the earliest heterogeneous integration in client computing, including glass-core substrates and early chiplet stacking. He brought that background to a deceptively simple question: how do you package VCSELs with CMOS in a way that's actually manufacturable?  His answer was fan-out wafer-level packaging (FOWLP) - a process already running at volume in your smartphone. He dropped III-V VCSEL die, photodiodes, a TIA and a driver into a standard OSAT flow, following existing design rules without inventing a single new process step. The result: a compact optic engine 17.5 x 5.6mm - roughly the size of a tic-tac - capable of 12.8 Tb/s across 80 channels at 50 Gbps per channel, at under 2.5 pJ/bit, with bandwidth density above 1.5 Tb/s per millimeter.  The engineering choices were deliberate. He wanted to de-risk everything downstream - fibre integration, board validation, test - so he kept the assembly flow completely conventional. That discipline is what allowed the team to debut the package at OFC earlier this year.  Matt's broader point was equally sharp: the photonics industry doesn't need to repeat the painful early years of advanced semiconductor packaging. The yield learning, the process infrastructure, the OSAT ecosystem - it already exists. The opportunity is to design optical solutions around what works, rather than starting from scratch.  On standards, he was pragmatic: wait 12 to 18 months. The field is too fragmented today to standardise usefully. When two or three approaches pull ahead, standards will follow naturally and will actually mean something.  On the VCSEL vs. external laser question: he was clear-eyed. VCSELs are not a universal answer. They don't have the reach for scale-across applications. But for within-rack and rack-to-rack, they offer a lower-cost, lower-power, high-bandwidth option that doesn't require reinventing the supply chain.  The photonics community tends to treat packaging as a downstream problem. Matt's work suggests it should be the starting point.

WHY CO-PACKAGED OPTICS FAILED IN 2011 - AND WHY IT WON'T THIS TIME. At the Optica GAMA Meeting last week (June 2-3 2026), Bert Jan Offrein of IBM Research Zurich opened with a question that cuts to the heart of where photonics stands today: why, after IBM deployed co-packaged optics in a supercomputer back in 2011, did the industry walk away from it for a decade? The answer matters because we're back at the same junction. Follow the link below to watch the 21-minute presentation on the Optica YouTube channel. https://lnkd.in/g56CA-mz.  In 2011, optics lost to a workaround. The ratio of communication to compute dropped from roughly one byte per flop to less than one hundredth. Electrical systems adapted, and the assembly overhead of optics - the lasers, modulators, drivers, alignment tolerances, fiber routing - simply wasn't worth it.  That trade-off no longer works. AI training and inference have reversed the pressure. We're back to multi-chip modules, back to bandwidth walls, and back to optics as the only credible path forward. But the overhead problem hasn't gone away.  Bert's argument is that solving it requires rethinking assembly, not just components. IBM's polymer waveguide approach - where light transitions adiabatically from a silicon or silicon nitride chip waveguide into a polymer matched to fiber mode size - enables 100 optical connections in a single flip-chip attach. That's the direction: optical connectivity that integrates into standard electrical assembly flows, is solder-reflow compatible, and is Telcordia-tested.  Further out, a Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA)-funded project is targeting 3D optical routing with 3-micron waveguide pitch, vertical vias, and chip-to-chip optical bonding through substrates - essentially replicating in the optical domain what the back-end-of-line does electrically.   Current mirror losses are around 1 dB and improving. The practical implication: the technology choices that will define the next generation of AI infrastructure won't be decided by peak performance specs. They'll be decided by who solves the assembly overhead at system level - not just the first-level package, but across the full board and chassis.  IBM Research is now operating as a collaborator rather than a licensor - open to partnerships that can bring these approaches to production. That shift in posture, from IP generator to system integrator, may matter as much as the technology itself.  Bert Jan Offrein presented at the Optica GAMA Meeting, June 2, 2026. Optica brings together the photonics community to accelerate exactly these kinds of industry-critical conversations. Why not join us? https://lnkd.in/g56CA-mz  hashtag #Photonics  hashtag #CoPackagedOptics  hashtag #AIInfrastructure  hashtag #Optica

WHAT HAPPENS WHEN A 50-POUND INSTRUMENT FITS ON A CHIP At the GAMA meeting in Brussels on June 3rd, Diana Mojahed, PhD, CEO of Optica Corporate member Lightfinder (based in Boston USA), closed the conference with something the room had not seen before. Lightfinder has built a chip-based optical spectrum analyzer on standard silicon photonics. No exotic materials. No moving parts. A device that fits in a small enclosure -- and delivers spectral resolution and bandwidth comparable to a Yokogawa OSA that weighs 50 pounds and costs tens of thousands of dollars per unit. The technology is a digital Fourier transform spectrometer: a cascade of Mach-Zehnder interferometers on a silicon photonic chip, using a single photodetector and a Fourier-domain encoding scheme to achieve very competitive resolution. Add one stage to the architecture, and you double your channel count -- an exponential scaling property that conventional dispersive spectrometers simply cannot match.  Why does this matter for our industry? Because co-packaged optics is heading toward volume production, and optical testing has not kept up. ATE companies can run 10,000 electronic tests simultaneously. The optical test is still stuck at one bench instrument per measurement point. LightFinder is building the chip that changes that ratio.  The Optica GAMA audience saw the potential immediately. By the end of the Q&A, there were several offers of a packaging and automation partnership from the floor. Beyond CPO, the platform roadmap covers extended NIR and visible wavelengths—spectroscopy-on-a-chip for semiconductor process control, medical diagnostics, remote sensing, and industrial inspection. The same silicon photonic infrastructure being built for datacenters could produce sensing chips at a volume that rivals CPO itself. If you are working in photonic packaging, test, or sensing -- and you are not already talking to LightFinder -- now is the time. Diana Mojahed can be reached at diana@lightfindertech.com. The intake form is at lightfindertech.com. Tell them Optica sent you. The video below is a slightly shorter version of the presentation on YouTube.

SABIC GRAND TOUR AND THE CHALLENGE OF REFLOW SOLDERING On our way to Belgium, Optica just visited SABIC's Opto-Electronic Centre of Excellence in Bergen op Zoom, The Netherlands. We got the full tour with Gabrie Hoogland and Young Joon Choi to understand how new materials can solve one problem that catches optical designers off guard: reflow soldering. The interview is 30 minutes and is therefore available on YouTube.   JEDEC protocols run at 260°C, three passes. Most thermoplastic resins deform at that temperature. Most optical designers discover this at the packaging stage, not the design stage.  SABIC developed Extem - a thermoplastic polyimide with a glass transition temperature of 280°C - specifically to close that gap. It survives reflow, can be injection moulded with 5nm surface roughness and sub-micron dimensional tolerances, and is capable of millions of parts per year. Three things stood out from the visit:  1. Alignment before reflow is now possible. Package your optics on the board first, then solder. That changes the economics for OSAT companies.  2. Epoxy is avoidable. Extem and Ultem can be metallised and self-aligned on a PCB via solder paste capillary action. No adhesive required.  3. Detachable connectors work. A demonstrator built with Tyndall National Institute showed 2.2 dB penalty over 40 make-and-break cycles using expanded beam microlens arrays. The loss came from the PIC design, not the lenses. So, if you are designing for co-packaged optics and have not checked your material's reflow compatibility, check now.  Interested in more information? Contact Gabrie Hoogland and tell them you saw the Optica interview. https://lnkd.in/g5z3kj-p

NANOIMPRINT LITHOGRAPHY FINDS ITS KILLER APPLICATION: CO-PACKAGED OPTICS Two conversations. One in Eindhoven, one at ITF2026 in Antwerp. Both pointed to the same conclusion: SCIL Nanoimprint Solutions is positioning itself at a critical junction in the co-packaged optics supply chain.  I spoke with James Quinn, CEO, at High Tech Campus Eindhoven, and with Erik Peters, Chief Commercial Officer, during imec's ITF2026 in mid-May 2026. The “NIL” technology has been around for 20 years. That is actually the point. Nanoimprint lithography has had two decades to mature — throughput is up, defect rates are down, and inorganic materials that survive reflow soldering at 260°C are now production-ready. What was missing was the application. Co-packaged optics is that application.  What SCIL brings is not just equipment. It is a combination of process know-how, proprietary materials, and an equipment platform — all developed in-house. The materials cure at room temperature via a purely chemical process, so there is no thermal distortion. The result: critical dimensions down to 10 nanometers, parabolic mirrors and lenses with the shape precision needed to hit target focal lengths, and optical patterns that survive the full back-end process, including reflow soldering, without shrinkage or degradation.  James Quinn put the timing bluntly: the next 18 to 24 months will set the supply chain for the next decade. Decisions are being made now, and SCIL is building the infrastructure to be in the room — a direct presence in Taiwan, an application centre planned for Singapore, and business development hiring underway in Silicon Valley. Erik Peters framed the product position clearly: SCIL Nanoimprint Solutions prints optical patterns directly on chip and on plugs to make fiber-to-chip a pluggable solution. The company is not just shipping equipment. It is running pre-production and pilot lines for customers while they qualify their devices — a deliberate strategy to become a design-win partner, not a component vendor.  The Philips lineage matters here. SCIL was incubated inside Philips for 15 to 20 years before spinning out. James Quinn described it as the second-largest lithography spinout from Philips, after ASML. That heritage shows in the depth of the materials IP and in the manufacturing discipline the team brings. The vision Quinn sketched — ASML handling HBM stacking, SCIL handling the photonics, both ending up on the same photonic interposer — is not a marketing slide. It reflects where the substrate roadmap is heading. imec.xpand is the lead investor, and Erik Peters was direct about the value: not just capital, but connections and technology direction. Being inside the imec ecosystem at a moment when the CPO supply chain is still being assembled is a structural advantage. The honest constraint: SCIL cannot be everywhere at once.   hashtag #photonics  hashtag #nanoimprint  hashtag #copackagedoptics  hashtag #CPO  hashtag #microoptics  hashtag #ITF2026  hashtag #semiconductors  hashtag #Eindhoven

300 GIGABITS PER SECOND PER CHANNEL, LIVE, ON THE IMEC SHOW FLOOR At  hashtag #ITF2026, we love to watch the demos. And imec was brave enough to run one live, in front of a packed crowd: a 300 Gb/s per-channel link. Anyone who has set up a 300 Gb/s measurement knows how fragile this is. Doing it surrounded by people, all day, is another level.  We spoke with Bart Moeneclaey, Principal Member of Technical Staff at imec during last week’s ITF2026 in Antwerp.  What he brought: a digital-to-analogue converter in 5nm FinFET, running at 150 Gsamples/s and generating a PAM4 signal, so 300 Gb/s of data transmission. The hard parts, in his words: bandwidth first, then power consumption and output swing. The classic trade-offs are pushed to the edge of what the process allows. When I asked if he was worried it would fail, his answer was practical: his colleague Yuri built the setup, it is solid, and as long as nobody touches the probes, it runs.  The honest point: this is the electronics. The real prize is the optical link. Driving a modulator and the full photonic path at 300 Gb/s per channel is a serious challenge, and imec is already working on modulator drivers and modulators with good results, even if they were not part of this demo.  Why it matters for the industry: with Marvell Technology's recent acquisition of Polariton Technologies Ltd. (announced 22 April 2026), the race for high-speed optical interconnect is consolidating fast. Anyone with a modulator that can carry 300 Gb/s per channel and wants to test it against a setup like this should know imec is open to collaboration. Thank you, Bart. That was excellent. And, as he kept reminding me: don't touch it.  hashtag #photonics  hashtag #imec  hashtag #ITF2026  hashtag #opticalinterconnect  hashtag #PAM4  hashtag #semiconductors

THE PCB COMPANY THAT RANG THE NASDAQ BELL AND IS NOW REWRITING THE RULES OF PHOTONICS INTEGRATION On Wednesday, May 27th 2026, Edwin Roks stood at the NASDAQ closing bell in New York. Earlier that same day, TTM Technologies had held a major investor meeting. The day after, I linked up with Edwin for a conversation that I think every company in the photonics industry needs to hear.  Edwin is President and CEO of TTM Technologies -- a global public company with over 20,000 employees and one of the world's leading manufacturers of advanced printed circuit boards. He is also a founding member of the Optica Executive Team of Trustees, which tells you something important: this is a PCB company that has decided photonics is not peripheral to its strategy. It is central to it.  The reason is straightforward. At 800 gigabit and 1.6 terabit data rates, electrical interconnects hit a fundamental wall. Loss, crosstalk, power consumption -- you cannot scale electrically to the channel densities that AI clusters require. So optical integration is moving from the edge of the board onto the board itself. TTM's data centre segment grew 57% year-on-year in Q4 2025. That growth is structural, not cyclical.  But the technical challenge is real. One of the things Edwin was direct about is the reflow soldering problem. Standard PCB assembly runs at 240 to 260 Celsius. Most photonics packaging was never designed to survive that thermal excursion. It was designed for the lab or for telecom rack environments. Getting optical components to perform reliably in high-volume PCB assembly requires the photonics and electronics manufacturing industries to stop running on parallel tracks and start co-designing from the beginning -- not at the qualification stage, but two or three years before production. On Europe: With NATO members under real pressure to build domestic defence electronics capacity, and with Europe's depth in photonics components and systems, Edwin sees the region as a genuine strategic priority, not just a market to serve.  Optica's role in this is also worth noting. The Forum in Malaga has become the place where these conversations move from strategy to action. Edwin will again be a speaker at the 2026 Optica Global Photonics Economic Forum on September 24 and 25 in Malaga.  If your company makes optical components, Edwin's message is direct: your product will be integrated onto a 120-150 layer PCB running through a reflow oven. Design for that from the start. The window to align your roadmap is shorter than most people in the photonics community realise. More details in the deep-tech interview below.

PHOTONIC COMPUTING JUST RAN ITS FIRST BILLION-PARAMETER LLM Last week, my colleague Jon Pugh visited Lumai at The Oxford Trust 's Wood Centre For Innovation in the UK and met with James Spall, co-founder and CTO, to take a closer look at what they are building. The conversation is worth your time.  Most of the photonics-in-data-centre discussion focuses on optical interconnect between GPUs or optical circuit switching. Lumai is doing something different: they are using light to perform the computation itself, not just move data between digital processors.   Their Lumai’s Iris Nova server system, announced last month, is the first optical computing system to run billion-parameter language models end-to-end. Not a lab demonstration. A rack-scale system processing real-world LLMs. The architecture has a scaling property that digital cannot match. Computational power grows quadratically with array size, while energy consumption grows only linearly. That means that as the system scales up, it gets faster and more energy-efficient at the same time. That is not an incremental improvement over GPU-based inference. It is a different physics.   The company is a spin-out from the University of Oxford and is now well beyond the research stage. Evaluations are open. If you are building or operating AI infrastructure, it is worth a conversation: check out lumai.ai. If you want to hear more directly from the Lumai team, they will be presenting at the Optica GAMA meeting in Brussels on 2-3 June 2026. PHOTONHUB HAS HELPED 100 COMPANIES GENERATE 750 MILLION EUROS IN NEW REVENUE. WITH 20 MILLION EUROS OF EU FUNDING. That is not a rounding error. That is a 37x return, and it says something important about where photonics value is actually being created right now.  My colleague Olga Raz recently spoke with Hugo Thienpont, research director at VUB B-PHOT Brussels Photonics and coordinator of PhotonHub, ahead of our GAMA Forum in Brussels on June 2 and 3. Photonhub is our gracious host for the meeting. The conversation covered ground I think the advanced manufacturing community needs to hear directly.  PhotonHub is a 33-partner consortium funded by the European Commission. Its core mission is not to serve photonics companies. It is to bring photonics into sectors that do not yet know they need it: agro-food, health, energy, space, and industrial manufacturing. That is where the 7% compound annual growth rate is coming from. Not photonics for the sake of photonics.  Hugo was direct about where companies get stuck. The first wall is awareness. Most SMEs simply do not know what photonics can do for their product margins or specifications. The second wall is access: no in-house experts, no fabrication platforms. The third is capital. A working prototype means nothing if you cannot fund the next step. PhotonHub's response to wall three is the European Photonics Venture Forum, which has channelled 250 million euros in VC into these companies and helped create 1,000 jobs.  On the prototype-to-production bottleneck, Hugo made a point worth sitting with. Most European manufacturers will not take a run of 100 or 200 components. The economics do not work for them, and SMEs cannot afford in-house scale-up. PhotonHub's pilot lines, covering indium phosphide, silicon photonics, silicon nitride, fiber fabrication and extreme optics, exist to bridge exactly that gap.  On the question of what photonics needs to compete at mass manufacturing scale, his answer was three things: sustained demand creation among non-photonics industries, serious capital deployment, and an ecosystem where photonics companies, RTOs, non-photonics end-users, VCs and policymakers are all in the same room making decisions together.  He also flagged Thema Foundries' 200 million euro investment in an indium phosphide chip manufacturing line in Belgium as the kind of move Europe needs more of. Targeting datacom transceivers for data centres is not a peripheral application. It is infrastructure. We will be discussing all of this at the Optica GAMA Forum in Brussels next week. A few seats remain. https://lnkd.in/e82yuPKD If you are working on photonics manufacturing, scaling a photonics-enabled product, or making investment or policy decisions in this space, this is the room to be in.
