OPTICAL DESIGN JUST GOT A LOT FASTER Last year at the Optica Global Photonics Economic Forum in Malaga, my colleague Olga Raz chatted with the founder of Fermat, a spin-off from VUB B-PHOT Brussels Photonics, to talk about what they are building. The company is now officially incorporated, and the timing feels right to share this conversation.  Fermat is doing something deceptively simple: applying mathematics, specifically Fermat's principle, to optical design. The result is a tool that can generate optical solutions from scratch in minutes rather than weeks. Their founder gave me a concrete example: a colleague ran the same design brief both ways. The traditional approach took one to two weeks. With Fermat's software, the same job was done in five minutes.  That is not a marketing claim. That is the kind of compression in development time that changes how optical design teams are structured and what they can take on. They are not positioning themselves as a replacement for optical designers. They want to make skilled designers faster, freeing up time for the work that actually requires human judgment. The near-term focus is on stock lens catalogs, the kind of component-based work that firms like Thorlabs and Edmund Optics supply to, which is technically demanding and commercially underserved by current software tools.  The broader opportunity sits anywhere cameras are deployed: automotive LiDAR, agricultural imaging, medical, space, and security, which has moved up every priority list in the past two years.  Fermat is also capital efficient by design. A relatively modest investment translates into increased headcount, faster software development, and immediate revenue. The return timeline is short. They are part of the PhotonHub network and will be at the Optica GAMA meeting in Brussels on 2-3 June 2026. If optical design or photonic systems are part of your business, it is worth a conversation.

TAIWAN IS NO LONGER JUST A MANUFACTURING HUB. IT IS THE OPERATIONAL CENTER OF THE AI ECONOMY. Jensen Huang made that plain this week in Taipei: Nvidia is now spending between US$100 billion and $150 billion annually across its Taiwan supply chain — up from $10–15 billion just three or four years ago. Headcount in Taiwan will grow from 1,000 to 4,000 by 2030, anchored by a new company campus in downtown Taipei called Constellation.  The numbers from AMD tell a parallel story. CEO Lisa Su announced $10 billion in Taiwan investments spanning chip packaging and substrate suppliers — a signal that the concentration of AI hardware capability on the island is not a Nvidia-specific bet, but an industry-wide structural commitment.  This is worth paying attention to for anyone building in deep tech. Taiwan's supply chain — TSMC, Quanta Energy, Foxconn and dozens of others — is not just executing someone else's roadmap. It is where AI supercomputers are designed, packaged, and assembled. Taiwan recently overtook India to become the world's fifth-largest stock market, driven in large part by this investment cycle.  On the question of AI and jobs, Huang was direct with his own employees: "You are not going to lose your job to AI. You are going to lose your job to someone who uses AI." His position is that AI adoption drives productivity, which drives hiring — not displacement.  The one constraint Huang flagged publicly, and directly to Taipei's mayor: energy. Taiwan needs to solve its electricity supply if it wants to sustain this trajectory. That problem is real across Asia, and it will shape which parts of the AI infrastructure buildout stay in Taiwan and which migrate elsewhere.  The COMPUTEX TAIPEI event next week will bring together CEOs from Intel, Arm, Marvell, Qualcomm and NXP — all in Taipei, all meeting the same supply chain partners. The geography of AI is consolidating, and it runs through Taiwan.  Source: Lauly Li and Cheng Ting-Fang, Nikkei Asia, May 27, 2026 https://asia.nikkei.com

WHERE INSTRUMENTS STOP, LEAPWAVE BEGINS One of the themes running through last week's imec ITF 2026 is that the frequency boundaries separating RF, microwave and photonics are collapsing. The engineering challenge is no longer just within one domain. It is how you connect them.  I chatted with Guillermo Carpintero, founder and CSO of LeapWave Technologies, a spin-off from his university group and the outcome of a European Pathfinder project that generated five patents in dielectric interconnects. The premise is straightforward. The high-frequency range for LeapWave starts at 100 GHz. That is roughly where the instruments available on the market today stop. LeapWave operates from 100 GHz through the terahertz range, and from there into the bandwidth of photonic devices themselves, which can reach hundreds of GHz but have lacked connectors or interfaces capable of reaching them. Until now. Their core technology is a dielectric interconnect that does not suffer the distance constraints of coaxial cables. At these frequencies, coaxial technology limits you to roughly a centimetre of cable length. LeapWave removes that constraint. That matters enormously for chiplet architectures, which was a central conversation across the whole event in Antwerp. What caught my attention was the announcement with their partner PHIX Photonics Assembly LeapWave has integrated a 6G antenna directly into a photonic device using the same dielectric technology. The result is an 8x8 antenna array capable of beam steering, broadband from 50 to 500 GHz, non-resonant, and able to emit multiple beams at different frequencies simultaneously. That is massive MIMO across the full spectrum, not just at a single frequency.  The testing angle is where this gets particularly relevant for the industry right now. Marvell Technology's acquisition of Polariton Technologies Ltd. made clear that leading companies are pushing into the 400 to 500 GHz range and beyond. The question nobody has fully answered is how you test at those frequencies, especially when your device has optical inputs on one side and electrical on the other. LeapWave Technologies' contactless probes have been continuously validated up to 500 GHz, and they are now combining them with optical periscopes on fibre, enabling combined optical and electrical probing in a single setup. That is a genuinely new capability.  Guillermo put it plainly: at these frequencies there are no standards, no established connectors, and real hesitation in the market about adopting something unfamiliar. LeapWave is doing the education work alongside the engineering work, which is the only way breakthrough technology actually gets used.  We will see them at the ECOC Exhibition, at the Optica Global Photonics Economic Forum (Sept 24/25th: Register now), and at European Microwave Week this autumn. If you are working anywhere near the boundary of high-speed electronics and photonics, they are worth a conversation.

OPTICS IS NOW THE CONNECTIVE TISSUE OF SEMICONDUCTOR PRODUCTION I've been to many ITF events. imec ITF 2026 felt different. When Jaihyuk Song , the CTO of Samsung Semiconductor stood up and showed co-packaged optics as an integral part of the CMOS production ecosystem, not a side project, not a roadmap item, but a core element of how chips get built at scale, I knew something had shifted.  That moment framed everything I saw at the many IMEC booths this year. imec launched a new programme called XTCO, cross-technology optimization. On paper it addresses compute density, power delivery, thermal management, memory bandwidth and optical fabric together, as a single co-design problem. In practice, it's the most serious attempt I've seen to break down the wall between photonics and semiconductor manufacturing.  I spent time with three people from the XTCO team. Dries Dictus explained the core logic: the era of pure logic scaling is slowing. The real gains now come from building bigger, better-connected systems. That requires solving five or six interdependent engineering challenges simultaneously, not sequentially. XTCO is the framework for doing that with partners like Samsung and TSMC before the foundry PDKs even exist.  imen jadli took me through where optics is heading inside that stack. Co-packaged optics is the near-term reality. But imec is already working on OIO3D, where photonics becomes a native layer in the 3D stack. That requires new materials, silicon germanium has limits, and the work on BTO and polymers is where the real frontier sits.  Peter Ossieur, who has spent his career pushing high-speed electronics and photonics closer together, laid out what fabric actually means at system level. The goal is to eliminate as much of the electronic-to-photonic interface as possible using hybrid bonding, place the optics directly inside the XPU-HBM stack, and then solve the thermal and power delivery consequences of that decision. The modelling and silicon-validated PDKs to support this are on track for deployment around 2030 to 2035.  My main takeaway from ITF 2026: optics is no longer adjacent to semiconductor production. It is the fabric that holds the whole system together. If you are a fabless company building AI compute hardware and you are not yet engaged with imec's XTCO programme, that is a gap worth closing.https://lnkd.in/d5HZw_cv
